title: The Inheritor
tagline: He didn't build the greatest empire the world had ever seen. He was born into it. Then he exceeded it.
hook_line: His father built the greatest empire the world had ever seen. Xerxes looked at it and decided it wasn't enough.

scenes.title: What He Was Born Into
scenes.narration: His father was Darius the Great. His grandfather was Cyrus the Great. Xerxes of Persia was born into the most powerful dynasty the ancient world had ever produced.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting style, aerial view of the Persian Empire at its height — Persepolis in foreground, empire stretching to every horizon in all directions, gold and amber light, impossibly vast, 50 nations visible as territories, dramatic cinematic composition, dark dramatic sky, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: 50 Nations
scenes.narration: The Persian Empire at its height: fifty nations under one crown. Egypt. Babylon. Assyria. Media. The largest empire the ancient world had ever seen.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, Xerxes enthroned at Persepolis receiving tribute from 50 nations simultaneously — ambassadors in distinct national dress from Egypt, Babylon, India, Ethiopia, Greece stretching in a great line before the throne, gold and amber throne room, overwhelming scale, dramatic lighting, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: His Father's Vow
scenes.narration: But Darius had failed at Marathon. He had vowed to conquer Greece and died before he could. That vow passed to Xerxes. He did not treat it as a suggestion.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, Darius the Great on his deathbed in Persian grandeur, hand extended toward the west, Xerxes standing at his side young and listening, the weight of inheritance passing between them, dramatic amber candlelight, solemn and cinematic, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: The Decision
scenes.narration: His generals advised patience. His advisors counseled delay. Xerxes spent four years preparing. Not because he hesitated. Because he did not intend to fail.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, Xerxes in war council with Persian generals and advisors, maps of Greece spread across a great table, Xerxes at the head — calm, absolutely certain, listening to advisors but already decided, dramatic directional lighting from above, gold and shadow, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: The Call Goes Out
scenes.narration: The summons went to every corner of the empire. Every nation under the Persian crown was required to send soldiers. From Ethiopia to India. From Egypt to Bactria.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting montage effect, Persian royal messengers riding in all directions simultaneously — west to Egypt, east to India, south to Ethiopia, north to Scythia — the empire mobilizing, dust rising across vast landscapes, amber sky, dramatic, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: They All Came
scenes.narration: They all came. Forty-seven nations. Warriors in every armor, speaking every language, carrying every weapon the ancient world had devised.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, vast Persian mustering ground showing warriors from 47 nations in perfect formation — Median cavalry, Egyptian infantry, Indian war elephants, Ethiopian archers, Scythian horsemen — all assembled under one banner, overwhelming diversity of the empire at war, dramatic wide shot, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: Persepolis at Full Power
scenes.narration: Persepolis had never seen anything like it. The greatest city in the world preparing the greatest army the world had ever assembled.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, Persepolis from above in full mobilization — armories open, supply columns moving through the great gates, cavalry drilling in formation on the plains, infantry in columns extending to the horizon, the city itself like a beating heart of war, dramatic aerial oil painting, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: What He Told His Generals
scenes.narration: 'We will not stop until our spears have been planted in every corner of Greece.' He did not say this with anger. He said it the way you state a fact.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting close-up of Xerxes addressing his war council, calm and absolutely certain, the directional light catching his crown and robes, generals in foreground listening with a mixture of reverence and fear, the composure of absolute power at rest, dramatic, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: The Greeks Hear
scenes.narration: The Greeks heard what was coming. Most of them surrendered immediately. They had counted the nations. They had done the math. They sent earth and water.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, Greek city representatives kneeling before Persian emissaries, presenting earth and water — the ancient symbols of surrender — in the foreground, while in the background the shadow of the approaching Persian army darkens the horizon, dramatic contrast of scale, 1080x1920 vertical

scenes.title: A Few Did Not
scenes.narration: A few did not. Sparta sent the Persian messengers into a well. Athens burned the Persian emissaries. Xerxes noted this. He continued preparing. He was not in a hurry.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic oil painting, split composition — left: Spartan warrior dropping Persian emissary into a well with cold contempt; right: Xerxes receiving news of this calmly, absolutely unmoved, a slight acknowledgment, continuing to plan — the composure of someone who has already decided the outcome, dramatic, 1080x1920 vertical

viral_hook: His father failed at Marathon. He spent four years preparing the response. Not because he hesitated — because he did not intend to fail.
highlight_note: 'We will not stop until our spears have been planted in every corner of Greece.' The calm delivery — fact, not threat — establishes Xerxes as something beyond a conqueror.
rewatch_bait: The final scene — Xerxes receiving news of Sparta's defiance and simply continuing to plan — becomes more chilling the more you know about what happened next.
youtube_title: His Father Built the Greatest Empire. Xerxes Looked at It and Decided It Wasn't Enough. | Gods & Glory
lesson: The most dangerous leaders are not the ones who act in anger. They are the ones who decide with absolute certainty and then prepare.
arc_notes: GG Historical S1 EP001 — establishes Xerxes as heir to impossible legacy. Sets up bridge episode (EP002), army scale (EP003), Thermopylae arc (EP005-EP009). Cross-feeds ML S1: after watching this, ML viewers understand the opponent their team faces.
